phase1.explanation:
Java is a high-level, object-oriented programming language created by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995.

The big idea behind Java is WORA — Write Once, Run Anywhere. You write code once, compile it into bytecode, and that bytecode runs on any machine with a Java Virtual Machine (JVM). It doesn't matter if you're on Windows, Mac, Linux, or a microwave with WiFi.

Java is:
• Object-Oriented — everything is built around objects and classes
• Strongly Typed — you must declare what type a variable is
• Garbage Collected — memory is managed for you
• Platform Independent — thanks to the JVM

In this course, we follow the CS22301 syllabus — OOP concepts first, then deeper into how Java makes OOP happen.
phase1.openingDialogue: Java was literally designed to run on a toaster~ Well, almost. Let me explain! ☕

phase2.explanation: This lesson is conceptual — no code to break yet! We'll get to that very soon. Phase 2 starts mattering from Lesson 1.3 onward~

phase3.prompt:
Quick check! Which of these is a Java feature?

A) Platform dependent — runs only on Windows
B) Uses pointers like C/C++
C) Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)
D) Manual memory management required
dialogueHints: Think about what makes Java special compared to C...
The JVM is the key. What does it make possible?
phase3.openingDialogue: Alright, time to see if you were listening~ 👀
